refined_fr.refined_fr: Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold throughout the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR 'achieve the customer-defined teleoperation link availability' is vague about the temporal and comparative nature of the requirement. The refined version clarifies that availability is a sustained property (not a one-time achievement) measured against a specific threshold over a defined period. This removes ambiguity about what 'achieve' means while remaining solution-neutral—it does not prescribe redundancy, failover mechanisms, or specific communication protocols. The FR is atomic: it expresses a single obligation (maintain availability above threshold) without embedded 'and'/'or' conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect teleoperation link uptime logs over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days of operational use). Calculate availability as (total_uptime / total_period). Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., 0.999). Pass if measured availability ≥ target; fail otherwise. Document any link outages, root causes, and recovery times.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic to maintain continuous link availability when primary pathway degrades or fails.
DP2: Deploy real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting to alternative pathways before availability threshold is breached.

DP3: Establish hierarchical communication architecture with local autonomous fallback mode that preserves critical teleoperation functions during link unavailability, resuming remote control upon link restoration.

DP4: Implement adaptive bandwidth allocation and compression strategies that maintain minimum viable teleoperation fidelity across variable link conditions, preventing threshold breach due to congestion.
DP5: Design communication protocol with built-in redundancy at packet level (forward error correction, retransmission logic) to recover from transient link degradation without pathway switching.

DP6: Establish service-level agreement enforcement through contractual link provisioning with carrier-backed availability guarantees and financial penalties, combined with monitoring to validate compliance.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP2 (integrated): Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic, augmented by real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting. This combination addresses both sudden failures (DP1) and gradual degradation (DP2), providing defense-in-depth without relying solely on external SLA enforcement (DP6) or local autonomy (DP3), which may not satisfy teleoperation continuity requirements.

potential_sources: DP1 and DP2 share dependency on common link quality telemetry; monitoring system must be decoupled from failover decision logic to avoid cascading failures.
DP1 failover logic and DP2 predictive rerouting may conflict if both trigger simultaneously; requires clear priority and arbitration rules.
Redundant pathways (DP1) and adaptive bandwidth (DP4) are loosely coupled; if combined, bandwidth adaptation must not mask link degradation signals needed by DP2.
DP1+DP2 assumes availability of multiple independent pathways; if constrained to single physical route, coupling to infrastructure becomes critical and unresolvable at DP level.
coupling_check.notes: DP1+DP2 integration is acceptable if: (1) monitoring and failover are architecturally separated; (2) arbitration rules are explicit; (3) pathway independence is verified at IM layer. Recommend explicit interface contract between monitoring subsystem and failover controller.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Multiple independent communication pathways are available or can be provisioned (e.g., cellular, satellite, wired backhaul). RISK: If worksite is geographically isolated or single-provider dependent, redundancy may be infeasible; mitigation requires DP3 (local fallback) or DP6 (SLA enforcement).

ASSUMPTION: Link quality can be measured and predicted with sufficient lead time (seconds to minutes) before availability threshold breach. RISK: Transient, unpredictable link faults may not allow proactive rerouting; mitigation requires DP5 (packet-level redundancy) as complementary strategy.

ASSUMPTION: Failover switching time is negligible relative to teleoperation control loop latency. RISK: If switching introduces >100ms latency, operator experience degrades; acceptance criteria may need revision to include latency bounds.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is representative of operational variability (weather, interference, traffic patterns). RISK: Short evaluation windows may not capture rare failure modes; recommend extended monitoring or statistical confidence intervals.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target threshold (0.999) is technically achievable with proposed DP. RISK: If empirical link availability is <0.999 due to infrastructure limitations, DP alone cannot satisfy FR; requires negotiation of realistic target or infrastructure upgrade.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation link is the only critical availability dependency. RISK: If other subsystems (power, sensor feeds, safety interlocks) have lower availability, overall system availability will be constrained by weakest link; FR scope may need clarification.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) acceptable failover latency and operator notification requirements; (2) whether 0.999 availability applies to end-to-end teleoperation or link layer only; (3) definition of 'link availability' (e.g., does brief packet loss count as unavailability?); (4) constraints on available pathways and infrastructure. Additionally, recommend decomposing FR into sub-FRs: FR1.1 (Detect link degradation), FR1.2 (Execute failover without operator intervention), FR1.3 (Maintain operator situational awareness during failover), FR1.4 (Log and report availability metrics). This enables independent DP evaluation and clearer IM ownership boundaries.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates redundant communication pathways, automatic failover logic, real-time link quality monitoring, predictive degradation detection, and proactive rerouting to maintain continuous teleoperation control channels under both sudden failures and gradual degradation scenarios.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface to physical/logical communication pathways; Teleoperation module monitors link quality metrics, detects degradation patterns, and triggers failover to alternate Network Link instances.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission directives; Teleoperation module ensures these are delivered reliably via redundant pathways with automatic rerouting on link failure.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and link quality metrics back to operator; Teleoperation module prioritizes critical telemetry over degraded links and adapts transmission strategy based on predicted link behavior.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals Safety Services when link quality falls below safe operation thresholds or failover is triggered; enables coordinated safety response independent of communication pathway selection.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: redundant pathways, failover logic, link quality monitoring, degradation detection, and rerouting are all core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: SafetyAlert interface is outbound only (safety coordination, not implementation detail); Network Link is a clean abstraction boundary. DP1+DP2 logic naturally resides here.

Poor containment: Vehicle module typically owns propulsion, sensing, and local autonomy. Communication redundancy and failover are not vehicle-specific concerns; they are operator-to-vehicle channel concerns. High diffusion: logic would leak into Teleoperation and Safety Services. Violates separation of concerns.

Poor containment: Safety Services owns safety policies and monitoring, not communication infrastructure. Redundant pathways and link quality prediction are not safety-specific; they are enablers of safe teleoperation. High diffusion: core DP logic would diffuse into Teleoperation and Network Link management. Conflates safety policy with communication mechanism.

Very poor containment: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not a functional module. Placing DP1+DP2 here would bypass architectural layering and create a monolithic, undecomposable boundary. Extreme diffusion: logic would scatter across all subsystems. Not a valid architectural choice.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: Teleoperation module (owned by teleoperation subsystem architect or communication/control integration team)
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural home for DP1+DP2 because it owns the operator-to-vehicle control channel and is responsible for ensuring command and feedback continuity. Redundant pathways, failover logic, and link quality monitoring are intrinsic to reliable teleoperation, not external concerns. The module's interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) cleanly separate communication mechanism (internal to Teleoperation) from safety policy (delegated to Safety Services via SafetyAlert) and vehicle dynamics (delegated to Vehicle via Tasking).
containment_risks: Risk: Network Link abstraction may not expose sufficient granularity for real-time link quality metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss, signal strength). Mitigation: Extend Network Link interface to include link quality telemetry (e.g., LinkQualityMetrics) as an inbound data stream.

Risk: Predictive degradation detection may require machine learning or statistical models that are computationally expensive. If Vehicle has limited onboard compute, this logic may need to reside in a ground station or edge gateway, creating a distributed DP. Mitigation: Clarify deployment topology (onboard vs. ground-based) and ensure Teleoperation module can coordinate with remote prediction services via well-defined interfaces.

Risk: Failover logic must be deterministic and low-latency to avoid operator-perceived delays. If Teleoperation module is implemented in a high-level language or framework with unpredictable scheduling, failover may be too slow. Mitigation: Specify real-time constraints and consider a dedicated failover state machine (possibly in firmware or a real-time kernel) as a sub-module within Teleoperation.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface is unidirectional (out). If Safety Services needs to veto a failover decision (e.g., reroute through an unsafe network), Teleoperation module cannot receive that feedback. Mitigation: Consider bidirectional SafetyAlert or a separate SafetyConstraint interface for safety-driven communication policy updates.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. DP1+DP2 is well-suited to the Teleoperation module boundary. However, to improve containment and reduce diffusion risk, recommend: (1) Explicitly define a LinkQualityMetrics sub-interface within Network Link to expose real-time and predictive link state to Teleoperation; (2) Clarify whether predictive degradation detection runs onboard (Vehicle) or ground-based (NGHE Solution), and if ground-based, define a separate PredictionService interface for Teleoperation to query; (3) Specify failover latency requirements and allocate failover logic to a deterministic, real-time sub-module within Teleoperation (e.g., FailoverController); (4) Consider adding a bidirectional SafetyConstraint interface to allow Safety Services to influence communication pathway selection without breaking Teleoperation's ownership of the mechanism.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (link availability ≥ 0.999 over agreed evaluation period). The Teleoperation IM boundary owns the mechanisms (redundant pathways, failover, link quality monitoring, predictive degradation detection, rerouting) required to achieve this target. Verification can be conducted via instrumentation of the Teleoperation module to log link state transitions, failover events, and availability metrics against the evaluation period. SafetyAlert interface provides traceability to safety-critical events. Constraint: Worksite safety plan and operational policies must be validated independently by Safety Services; Teleoperation module must respect safety-driven communication constraints via the recommended bidirectional SafetyConstraint interface.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to the Teleoperation IM with well-defined external interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert). The DP (DP1+DP2) is logically cohesive and naturally contained within this boundary. Ownership is unambiguous: teleoperation subsystem architect or communication/control integration team. However, allocation clarity is contingent on resolving three sub-allocation questions: (1) Where does predictive degradation detection execute (onboard Vehicle or ground-based NGHE Solution)? (2) What is the failover latency requirement, and which sub-module (firmware, real-time kernel, or application-level state machine) owns the FailoverController? (3) Does SafetyAlert remain unidirectional, or is a bidirectional SafetyConstraint interface required? These should be documented in the detailed design phase.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP (DP1+DP2 integrated) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. DP1 (redundant pathways + automatic failover) handles sudden link failures; DP2 (real-time monitoring + predictive degradation + proactive rerouting) handles gradual degradation. Together, they provide defense-in-depth without relying solely on external SLA enforcement (DP6) or local autonomy (DP3), which are insufficient for continuous teleoperation. The DP is implementable, testable, and measurable against the acceptance criterion (link availability ≥ 0.999). No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Risk: LinkQualityMetrics sub-interface may not be fully specified. Network Link abstraction must expose real-time metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss, signal strength) and predictive state (degradation probability, estimated time-to-failure) to Teleoperation. If Network Link only exposes binary up/down status, Teleoperation cannot implement DP2 (predictive degradation detection). Mitigation: Extend Network Link interface specification to include LinkQualityMetrics as a mandatory inbound data stream.

Risk: Predictive degradation detection logic may be distributed across Teleoperation (onboard) and a ground-based prediction service (NGHE Solution). If the prediction service is not formally owned by Teleoperation module, DP2 logic diffuses across module boundaries. Mitigation: If prediction is ground-based, define a PredictionService interface owned by Teleoperation and implement a clear contract (request/response, latency SLA, fallback behavior) to maintain logical cohesion of DP2.

Risk: Failover logic must be deterministic and low-latency (sub-100ms typical for teleoperation). If Teleoperation module is implemented in a high-level language or framework with unpredictable scheduling, failover latency may exceed requirements. Mitigation: Allocate failover logic to a dedicated, real-time sub-module (FailoverController) within Teleoperation with explicit latency constraints and deterministic scheduling guarantees.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface is unidirectional (out). If Safety Services detects an unsafe communication pathway and needs to veto a failover decision, Teleoperation cannot receive that feedback. This creates a potential safety gap: Teleoperation may reroute through a pathway that Safety Services has flagged as unsafe. Mitigation: Add a bidirectional SafetyConstraint interface to allow Safety Services to communicate communication-pathway safety policies to Teleoperation without breaking Teleoperation's ownership of the failover mechanism.

Risk: Redundant communication pathways may include diverse network types (cellular, satellite, mesh, wired) with different latency, bandwidth, and reliability characteristics. Teleoperation module must abstract these differences and present a unified interface to Tasking and Telemetry consumers. If pathway-specific logic leaks into Teleoperation, containment degrades. Mitigation: Implement a NetworkPathway abstraction layer within Teleoperation that encapsulates pathway-specific behavior and exposes a uniform interface to failover and rerouting logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). DP1+DP2 logic is naturally and cohesively contained within the Teleoperation module. Redundant pathways, failover logic, link quality monitoring, degradation detection, and rerouting are all intrinsic to reliable teleoperation and do not belong in Vehicle (propulsion/sensing), Safety Services (safety policy), or NGHE Solution (system container). External interfaces are clean: Network Link is a mechanism abstraction, Tasking and Telemetry are data flows, SafetyAlert is a coordination signal. Minimal diffusion risk provided that: (1) LinkQualityMetrics is formally exposed by Network Link, (2) Predictive degradation detection is logically owned by Teleoperation (even if executed remotely), (3) Failover logic is allocated to a deterministic sub-module with specified latency constraints, and (4) SafetyAlert is augmented with a bidirectional SafetyConstraint interface for safety-driven communication policy updates.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Extend Network Link interface specification to include a mandatory LinkQualityMetrics sub-interface. Define the schema for real-time metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss, signal strength, link state) and predictive metrics (degradation probability, estimated time-to-failure, recommended reroute window). This ensures Teleoperation can implement DP2 (predictive degradation detection) without diffusing link quality logic into Network Link management.

Revision 2: Clarify the deployment topology for predictive degradation detection. If prediction runs onboard Vehicle, allocate it as a sub-module within Teleoperation and specify compute/memory constraints. If prediction runs ground-based (NGHE Solution), define a PredictionService interface with explicit contract (request/response format, latency SLA, fallback behavior when service is unavailable). Ensure Teleoperation logically owns the DP2 decision logic even if prediction is remote.

Revision 3: Specify failover latency requirements (e.g., ≤ 100ms for operator-perceived responsiveness) and allocate failover logic to a dedicated, real-time sub-module (FailoverController) within Teleoperation. Document the scheduling guarantees (e.g., real-time kernel, firmware-based state machine) required to meet latency constraints. This prevents failover logic from diffusing into high-level application code with unpredictable scheduling.

Revision 4: Add a bidirectional SafetyConstraint interface to allow Safety Services to communicate communication-pathway safety policies to Teleoperation. Define the contract: Safety Services can flag pathways as unsafe (e.g., due to proximity to hazardous zones), and Teleoperation must respect these constraints when selecting failover pathways. This prevents Teleoperation from rerouting through unsafe pathways and maintains safety-communication coordination without breaking Teleoperation's ownership of the failover mechanism.

Revision 5: Document the NetworkPathway abstraction layer within Teleoperation that encapsulates pathway-specific behavior (e.g., cellular vs. satellite latency profiles, bandwidth limitations, reliability characteristics). Ensure failover and rerouting logic operates on the abstraction, not on pathway-specific details, to maintain cohesion and prevent diffusion of pathway-specific logic into Teleoperation's core DP1+DP2 implementation.

Revision 6: Define acceptance test plan for link availability measurement. Specify: (a) instrumentation points within Teleoperation to log link state transitions, failover events, and availability metrics; (b) evaluation period duration and sampling frequency; (c) how availability is calculated (e.g., uptime / total time, accounting for planned maintenance); (d) how DP1 (sudden failure recovery) and DP2 (gradual degradation handling) are independently validated. Ensure test plan is traceable to the FR acceptance criterion (≥ 0.999 availability).

decision.justification: The FR-DP-IM gate passes all three checks (FR_IM: pass, FR_DP: pass, DP_IM: pass), and containment is excellent (0.92 score). However, the gate results identify six critical revisions required before decomposition can proceed safely. These revisions are not minor clarifications—they address fundamental architectural decisions that directly impact safety, latency, and module cohesion: (1) LinkQualityMetrics interface specification is incomplete, blocking DP2 implementation; (2) predictive degradation detection ownership is ambiguous (onboard vs. ground-based), risking diffusion; (3) failover latency requirements and real-time scheduling guarantees are unspecified, creating safety and performance risks; (4) SafetyAlert is unidirectional, creating a safety gap where Teleoperation may reroute through unsafe pathways; (5) NetworkPathway abstraction is not formally defined, risking pathway-specific logic leakage; (6) acceptance test plan is not detailed enough to verify the 0.999 availability criterion. Given the high safety criticality of this FR (teleoperation link availability directly impacts operator control and worksite safety), these revisions must be resolved at the current level before decomposing into sub-FRs. Proceeding to sub-FRs without resolving these would propagate ambiguity and safety risks into lower levels.

revise.why: Six critical revisions are required: (1) Extend Network Link interface to include mandatory LinkQualityMetrics sub-interface with real-time (latency, jitter, packet loss, signal strength) and predictive metrics (degradation probability, time-to-failure). (2) Clarify predictive degradation detection deployment: if onboard, allocate as Teleoperation sub-module with compute constraints; if ground-based, define PredictionService interface with explicit contract and latency SLA. (3) Specify failover latency requirement (e.g., ≤100ms) and allocate failover logic to dedicated FailoverController sub-module with real-time scheduling guarantees. (4) Add bidirectional SafetyConstraint interface to allow Safety Services to veto unsafe failover pathways, closing the safety gap. (5) Formally define NetworkPathway abstraction layer within Teleoperation to encapsulate pathway-specific behavior and prevent diffusion. (6) Develop detailed acceptance test plan specifying instrumentation points, evaluation period, availability calculation method, and independent validation of DP1 (sudden failure) and DP2 (gradual degradation). These revisions must be completed and re-gated before sub-FR decomposition.

assumptions: Assumption A1: Link availability ≥0.999 is achievable through redundant pathways and failover logic without requiring external SLA enforcement alone (DP6 insufficient).
Assumption A2: Teleoperation module owns the failover decision logic and is responsible for respecting safety-driven communication constraints communicated by Safety Services.
Assumption A3: Network Link abstraction can expose real-time and predictive quality metrics without requiring Teleoperation to directly manage network infrastructure.
Assumption A4: Predictive degradation detection can be logically owned by Teleoperation even if executed remotely (ground-based), provided a formal PredictionService interface is defined.

Assumption A5: Failover latency of ≤100ms is acceptable for operator-perceived responsiveness in teleoperation; if tighter latency is required (e.g., ≤50ms), failover logic must be firmware-based or kernel-level.
Assumption A6: Safety Services can communicate pathway safety policies to Teleoperation via a bidirectional SafetyConstraint interface without creating circular dependencies or deadlock conditions.

Assumption A7: Redundant pathways include diverse network types (cellular, satellite, mesh, wired) with heterogeneous latency and reliability profiles that can be abstracted by a NetworkPathway layer.
open_risks: Risk R1 (HIGH): LinkQualityMetrics interface not formally specified. If Network Link only exposes binary up/down status, Teleoperation cannot implement DP2 (predictive degradation detection). Impact: DP2 logic may diffuse into Network Link or be abandoned, reducing availability to reactive failover only. Mitigation: Extend Network Link interface specification before sub-FR decomposition.

Risk R2 (HIGH): Predictive degradation detection ownership ambiguous. If prediction service is ground-based and not formally owned by Teleoperation, DP2 logic diffuses across module boundaries. Impact: Unclear responsibility for prediction accuracy, latency, and fallback behavior. Mitigation: Clarify deployment topology and define PredictionService interface with explicit contract.

Risk R3 (HIGH): Failover latency requirements unspecified. If failover logic is implemented in high-level language with unpredictable scheduling, latency may exceed operator tolerance (typically ≤100ms). Impact: Operator perceives control lag, safety risk increases. Mitigation: Specify failover latency requirement and allocate logic to deterministic FailoverController sub-module.

Risk R4 (CRITICAL): SafetyAlert is unidirectional. If Safety Services detects unsafe pathway and Teleoperation reroutes through it anyway, safety violation occurs. Impact: Teleoperation may bypass safety constraints, creating hazard. Mitigation: Add bidirectional SafetyConstraint interface to allow Safety Services to veto pathways.

Risk R5 (MEDIUM): NetworkPathway abstraction not formally defined. If pathway-specific logic (e.g., cellular vs. satellite latency profiles) leaks into failover/rerouting logic, containment degrades and maintenance complexity increases. Impact: Difficult to add new pathway types or modify existing ones. Mitigation: Define NetworkPathway abstraction layer with uniform interface.

Risk R6 (MEDIUM): Acceptance test plan incomplete. If availability measurement methodology is not detailed (instrumentation points, evaluation period, calculation method), verification of 0.999 criterion may be ambiguous or non-reproducible. Impact: Acceptance criteria not verifiable. Mitigation: Develop detailed test plan before sub-FR decomposition.
evidence_needed: Evidence E1: Network Link interface specification document including LinkQualityMetrics sub-interface schema (real-time metrics: latency, jitter, packet loss, signal strength, link state; predictive metrics: degradation probability, estimated time-to-failure, recommended reroute window).

Evidence E2: Deployment topology decision for predictive degradation detection (onboard Vehicle vs. ground-based NGHE Solution) with justification (compute constraints, latency requirements, fault tolerance).
Evidence E3: PredictionService interface specification (if ground-based) including request/response format, latency SLA, fallback behavior when service unavailable, and error handling.

Evidence E4: Failover latency requirement specification (e.g., ≤100ms for operator responsiveness) and FailoverController sub-module design with scheduling guarantees (real-time kernel, firmware-based state machine, or equivalent).
Evidence E5: SafetyConstraint interface specification (bidirectional) defining contract between Safety Services and Teleoperation for pathway safety policy communication and veto mechanism.

Evidence E6: NetworkPathway abstraction layer design document specifying uniform interface for failover/rerouting logic and encapsulation of pathway-specific behavior (cellular, satellite, mesh, wired).

Evidence E7: Detailed acceptance test plan including: (a) instrumentation points within Teleoperation module; (b) evaluation period duration and sampling frequency; (c) availability calculation method (uptime / total time, accounting for planned maintenance); (d) independent validation strategy for DP1 (sudden failure recovery) and DP2 (gradual degradation handling); (e) traceability to FR acceptance criterion (≥0.999 availability).
Evidence E8: Safety analysis (FMEA or equivalent) documenting how bidirectional SafetyConstraint interface prevents unsafe rerouting and maintains safety-communication coordination.
